questions.prompt: Why are Supabase migrations forward-only by default?

choices.text: Down-migrations are tricky in practice: dropping a column loses data permanently; renaming back can break already-deployed code; the half-applied state during rollback is often worse than rolling forward. Forward-only forces you to think about safe schema changes upfront and write corrective migrations when something needs to be undone. If a bad migration ships, you write a new migration to fix it — don't try to undo by deleting migration files from history.

questions.explanation: Forward-only avoids the worst-case rollback scenarios; write corrective migrations.

questions.prompt: Adding a NOT NULL column to a 5M-row production table. Safe pattern?

choices.text: Three-step migration: (1) add the column as nullable (no lock issues, fast), (2) backfill values in batches (UPDATE in chunks of 1000-10000 rows, via a loop or pg_cron job — avoids locking the whole table), (3) once backfilled, alter the column to NOT NULL. Pair with `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY` for any new index so it doesn't block writes. The pattern: additive first, fill safely, tighten constraints last. Always test on a staging branch database before applying to prod.

questions.explanation: Add nullable → batched backfill → set NOT NULL. CONCURRENTLY for indexes.
